text: Robotic total pelvic exenteration is demonstrated for locally advanced rectal cancer. Multiple ports are visibly placed in the abdominal wall for robotic instrument access.

The surgeon creates a dissection plane beneath the inferior mesenteric vein, followed by medial to lateral mobilization of the rectal tube through medial peritoneal cuts. The rectosigmoid and descending colon are reflected off the retroperitoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected to its root, clipped, and divided beyond the origin of the left colic artery.

The surgeon performs posterior pelvic dissection of the rectal tube, completing lateral peritoneal cuts over the external iliac vessels to facilitate lateral pelvic lymph node dissection. The external iliac vessels are identified and dissected, while the ureter is dissected and shifted medially. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced to the internal iliac vasculature, creating a paravascular space. Nodal tissue between the obturator internus and the internal iliac vasculature is dissected and removed.

The surgeon addresses the medial nodal zone, located between the internal iliac vasculature laterally and the pelvic hypogastric medially. A space is created between these structures through gentle dissection, and the nodal tissue medial to the internal iliac vasculature is carefully dissected off the internal iliac vessel branches and removed.

The surgeon clips and divides the superior vesicle pedicle, followed by the blood vessels supplying the prostate and seminal vesicles. The left ureter is clipped and divided near the bladder, then dissected off the retroperitoneum for ureter entry anastomosis. The right lateral pelvic node dissection is performed similarly, with the right ureter clipped, divided, and mobilized. The bladder is mobilized, both umbilical arteries are divided, and the bladder is taken down by entering the space of Retzius.

The surgeon identifies, clips, and divides the dorsal venous complex. The prostate is dissected and lifted off the pelvic floor. Bleeding from the dorsal venous complex is controlled, and the area is packed to maintain a clean surgical field.

The surgeon divides the descending colon using an endostapler and clips the descending colon mesentery to the anterior abdominal wall. The ileocecal junction is mobilized to facilitate the placement of the ileal conduit. Umbilical tape measures the bowel length, and clips mark the proximal and distal ends of the conduit. Endo staplers are used for bowel division.

The surgeon establishes bowel continuity by performing a side-to-side ilio-iliostomy using endo staplers. The cut ends of the ureters are spatulated, and their medial walls are joined with absorbable suture material. Digest ends are inserted into both ureters using a guide wire, and the other ends are placed into the distal end of the ileal conduit.

The surgeon completes the urethra anastomosis using continuous absorbable sutures. The final excision is performed via the perineal route. Subsequently, the ileal conduit and end colostomy are fashioned.
